title: Implicit Type Conversion
html:
Implicit Type Conversion

  • Last UpdatedSept 19, 2024

    • 10.4

Implicit Type Conversion

Uniface implicitly converts the data types in the following circumstances:

  • Assigning a field, variable, or parameter to one with a different data type. For example, the command assigns a string to Time field. 
    TIMEFIELD = "123456"

    In this case, Uniface converts the string to the Time value 12:34:56

    For more information, see Assignments.

  • Comparing non-matching data types. For example, the following command compares a string with a Date field: 
    if ("12 jan 2019" < DATEFIELD)

    Uniface to converts the string to a Date value. If the implicit conversion is successful, the comparison is valid. For more information, see Expressions and Conditions.

  • Substituting in string constants. For example, the following statement converts the value of Numeric variable varTotal to a string that is displayed in a message: 
    message "varTotal is %%varTotal%%%"

    For more information, see Substitution in String Values.

  • Sending data from and to the presentation layer, whether this is provided by Dynamic Static Server Page components, or Form and Report components.

    For example, the display format of a Date field (which is internally stored as 20190331) may specify that the data be displayed as 31 March 2019. For more information, see Display Formats.

  • Converting data to and from XML. This can have unintended consequences. For example, Null fields are saved into XML streams as empty elements. This means that a null field is indistinguishable from an empty string. When an empty element is loaded into a numeric field it is converted into a zero numeric value. For more information, see Null Values.

In some circumstances, it is better to explicitly convert data using ProcScript. For example:

  • When comparing any non-String type to a String type, display format definitions must be taken into consideration because these are used when converting Strings. If possible, it is best to convert into types that are as specific as possible, removing any formatting issues.

    The following example shows that effect the NLS formatting applied in a String has when the data is converted to a number.

    variables
 string vString
 numeric vNumber
endvariables
$nlslocale = "de_DE" ; decimal separator is comma
$nlsformat = "nlslocale"
vString = "5,03" ; formatted string 
vNumber = vString ; vNumber=5 
vNumber = $number(vString) ; vNumber=5.03 
vString = "5.03"
vNumber = vString ; –> 5.03
vNumber = $number(vString) ; –> 503

    For more information, see Conversion to String.

  • When testing conditions, you should not rely on implicit type conversion to use strings as if they were Booleans. For example, if (vBoolean = "True") will work because the Boolean value is implicitly converted to a string beginning with T, which is interpreted as True. But so will if (vBoolean = "Thomas"). In this case, it is better to use the construction if (vBoolean) because it does not require conversion.
  • When a variable has no predefined type and the source data is a different type from that required for the target, it may be necessary to use some of the conversion functions.

For more information, see Explicit Type Conversion.

Was this topic helpful?
